name: Eternia
classification_notes: Tall proportions, semi-condensed width and controlled contrast place it squarely in the grotesque sans-serif tradition, with a vertical emphasis reminiscent of headline typefaces from 1970s–80s technology and advertising print.
summary: A semi-condensed grotesque drawn from the headline typography of 1970s and 1980s technology magazines and advertising print.
description: Tall proportions and slightly tight spacing give Eternia the dense, vertical texture of period headlines without tipping into outright pastiche. Controlled contrast and a semi-condensed width make it compact enough for heavy display use while remaining readable across weights from ExtraLight to Black. Sixteen styles include matching italics and variable font files for both roman and italic axes.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Eternia as inspired by the visual world of technology magazines, newspaper supplements and advertising typography from the 1970s and 1980s. Its tall proportions and semi-condensed width create a compact, vertical presence, while controlled contrast and slightly tight spacing recreate the density and texture of headlines from that era. The result, they say, feels familiar without becoming nostalgic, a typeface that could have existed decades ago and still feel relevant today.

name: Geogrotesque
classification_notes: Geogrotesque combines a semi-modular geometric construction with subtle rounded terminals, placing it at the intersection of geometric and grotesque sans-serif traditions. The optical adjustments and warm finish distinguish it from purely mechanical geometric sans-serifs.
summary: A semi-modular geometric sans with subtly rounded terminals, designed for display and intermediate-length text across seven weights.
description: Built on a consistent formal principle with optical corrections that allow the system to function as a workable alphabet, Geogrotesque sits between geometric rigidity and approachable warmth thanks to its rounded finish. Fourteen fonts cover seven weights from Thin to Bold, each with a matching italic, with the lighter cuts particularly intended for large-scale display use. Real-world adoption spans sports, editorial and institutional contexts, including Puma, Sports Illustrated and One World Observatory.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Geogrotesque as a semi-modular typeface with a subtle rounded finish, where all characters share the same formal construction principle with optical adjustments to function as a text alphabet. Despite its geometric or technological construction, the rounded finish gives it a warm, approachable quality. It was conceived primarily for display use in publications and intermediate-length texts, with the Thin and Ultralight weights specifically intended for large sizes.

name: Ciutadella
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a geometric sans serif with clean, simple forms and an open personality. Alternate two-story 'a' and consistent geometric construction place it firmly in the geometric sans subcategory.
summary: A geometric sans originally commissioned by Mario Eskenazi's studio, built across seven weights with italics and variable font support for both identity and publication work.
description: Originally commissioned by Mario Eskenazi's studio, this geometric sans prioritises simplicity and directness across a 16-style family spanning ExtraLight to ExtraBold with matching italics. Variable Roman and Variable Italic fonts extend the range for flexible typesetting. Though conceived primarily as a display face, an alternate two-story 'a' and open forms give it enough legibility for intermediate body text.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Ciutadella as a versatile geometric sans serif that is simple, clean and direct, originally commissioned by Mario Eskenazi's studio. They note its power lies in overarching simplicity and an open personality that suits a wide range of applications from identity systems to publications, and that while designed as a display typeface it performs well in shorter body text partly due to the alternate two-story 'a'.

name: Approach
classification_notes: A low-contrast sans-serif in the tradition of early grotesques, with utilitarian construction and mechanical details. The distinctive 'elbow pipe' terminals align it closely with nineteenth-century grotesque models while the synthetic punctuation adds a contemporary edge.
summary: A modern reinterpretation of early grotesques with a distinctive 'elbow pipe' terminal detail running across many characters, available in eight weights with matching italics.
description: Built on the bones of nineteenth-century grotesque tradition, the design introduces a recurring 'elbow pipe' shape in letters such as a, f, j, t, R, Q and 1, giving the family a consistent mechanical character. Sixteen styles span Light through UltraBlack with matching italics throughout. Synthetic punctuation and quotes sharpen the contemporary feel without breaking the familiar grotesque rhythm.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Approach as a modern approximation of early grotesques: a utilitarian, low-contrast typeface that is slightly mechanical but full of character. Its signature element is an elbow-pipe shape appearing in multiple letters and numerals, while synthetic punctuation and quotes lend it a more contemporary appearance. The aim is a typeface that feels fresh while remaining familiar.

name: Steradian
classification_notes: Steradian is built on a geometric base with a double-story 'a', closed apertures and distinctive capitals, placing it firmly in the geometric sans tradition while retaining optical adjustments that distinguish it from pure geometry.
summary: A geometric sans with a double-story 'a', closed apertures and idiosyncratic capitals such as the G and Q, available in eight weights with matching italics and variable font versions.
description: Built on a geometric foundation, Steradian diverges from genre convention by keeping letter widths relatively consistent across weights, a consequence of developing proportions from the heavier cuts downward. The double-story 'a', closed apertures and distinctive capitals give the family a quiet character that lifts it above generic geometric offerings. The family ships as 18 static fonts plus variable Roman and Italic, and has found use across cultural institutions, brand identities and editorial projects.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Steradian as an exploration of the geometric genre, noting that its letter widths remain unusually consistent across weights because the proportions of heavier weights informed the lighter ones. Key features cited include the double-story 'a', closed apertures and capitals with a distinct personality, particularly the G and Q.

name: Decagram
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a neo-grotesque with Swiss style influence, placing it firmly in the grotesque sans-serif tradition with industrial undertones rather than humanist or geometric tendencies.
summary: A neo-grotesque workhorse with Swiss style roots and industrial character, offered across 10 weights with matching italics and a variable font.
description: Built on a high x-height and loose width proportions with compact verticals, Decagram sits between Swiss rationalism and a more industrial sensibility. Distinctive details include a high vertex in the M, a narrow J, rounded punctuation dots and sporty figures that prevent it from feeling entirely neutral. The 21-style family, including a variable font, covers a wide weight range from Thin to UltraBlack with full italic support throughout.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Decagram as a modern sans serif blending Swiss style with an industrial hint, designed as a versatile neo-grotesque workhorse. It features high x-height, loose width proportions and compact vertical ones, with distinctive details such as a high vertex in the M, a narrow J, rounded dot punctuation and sporty figures. The family includes 10 weights plus italics and a variable font.

name: Akkordeon
classification_notes: Described as a display font family inspired by grotesques from the XIX and XX centuries, with variable breadth from narrow to expanded. The emphasis on compact, solid headline use places it firmly in the display category with grotesque roots.
summary: A 15-style display family drawing on 19th and 20th century grotesques, built around a variable breadth from narrow to expanded rather than a conventional weight axis.
description: Rather than stepping through weights in the usual manner, Akkordeon sequences its 15 styles across a continuous gradation of widths, from tightly compressed to fully expanded, giving each step a distinct typographic character. Inspired by grotesques from the XIX and XX centuries, the family is built for short-burst settings: magazine titles, book covers, banners, advertising and branding. A variable font version is available alongside individual style purchases.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Akkordeon as a display font family roughly inspired by grotesques from the XIX and XX centuries, not conceived as a family of constant width but offering a wide gradation of breadth from narrow to expanded. It is intended for short texts such as magazine titles, banners, book covers, charts, advertising and branding, wherever a compact, solid and powerful font is needed.

name: Aribau Grotesk
classification_notes: A hybrid of geometric and grotesque traditions, combining low contrast and generous proportions with American gothic traits such as open counters and a double-storey 'g', placing it firmly in the grotesque subcategory.
summary: A hybrid grotesque that blends American gothic traits with geometric details across eight weights and matching italics, including variable font support.
description: Drawing on early 20th-century American gothic conventions, Aribau Grotesk pairs open counters and a double-storey 'g' with the cleaner, more contemporary touches of geometric design: circular dots and clean-shaped figures. The family runs across eight weights from Thin to Black with matching italics, totalling 18 static styles, and variable Roman and Italic fonts are included with the full family purchase. Its generous proportions and low contrast make it a practical choice for editorial and branding work where a grounded but modern grotesque is needed.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Aribau Grotesk as born from the intersection of geometric and grotesque typefaces, combining low contrast and generous width proportions with American gothic traits from the early 20th century, such as open counters and a double-storey 'g'. They note that the design process introduced geometric details including clean-shaped figures and circular dots, lending the typeface a more affable and contemporary character. Variable fonts are included with the full family or available as separate styles.

name: Geogrotesque Sharp
classification_notes: A geometric grotesque superfamily drawing on the Geogrotesque lineage, with ink traps and rounded corners removed for a cleaner, more neutral appearance suited to screen use.
summary: A 99-style geometric grotesque superfamily spanning seven widths and a variable font, refined from the original Geogrotesque by stripping out ink traps and rounded corners.
description: Built on a decade of work, Geogrotesque Sharp takes the existing Geogrotesque design and simplifies its more idiosyncratic details to produce a cleaner, more versatile face. Seven widths run from XCompressed through to Expanded, each in seven weights from Thin to Bold with matching italics, totalling 98 static styles plus a variable font. The removal of ink traps and rounded corners shifts the family toward screen and web use while preserving the underlying geometric structure.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Geogrotesque Sharp as a superfamily that brings together a decade of work across seven widths and 99 styles. They note that some design aspects have been simplified without losing the typeface's character, with ink traps and rounded corners removed to make the family more usable and less idiosyncratic. A variable font version is included with the full family purchase or available as a separate style, reflecting the format's growing popularity for web use.

name: Ciutadella Rounded
classification_notes: Ciutadella Rounded is a geometric sans-serif distinguished by true rounded terminals across all weights rather than merely clipped corners, placing it firmly in the rounded geometric sans category.
summary: A geometric sans with true rounded terminals engineered to maintain that rounding consistently across all five weights, including the heaviest.
description: Rather than simply clipping corners, Ciutadella Rounded uses genuine rounded terminals throughout, a technical challenge the foundry note is particularly acute in heavy weights where tight counters leave little room for the round endings. Ten styles span Light to Bold with matching italics. The family suits identity systems, editorial work and anywhere a softer, more approachable tone is needed without sacrificing geometric structure.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Ciutadella Rounded as more than a font with soft corners, noting it features real rounded terminals across all weights. They highlight the difficulty of achieving a consistent rounded effect in close-counter characters, especially at the heaviest weights, and position the family as suitable across a wide range of applications from identity systems to publications.

name: Micrograph
classification_notes: A clean, mechanical sans serif with tight spacing and compact vertical proportions, consistent with geometric sans traditions while leaning toward a neutral, technical aesthetic.
summary: A mechanical sans serif with tight spacing, compact proportions and a clean aesthetic that sits between classical and contemporary models.
description: Designed with very short descenders and a compact vertical scale, it maintains legibility across a wide weight range from Thin to Black. A notable detail is the prominent punctuation in lighter weights that recedes as weight increases, adding quiet personality without disrupting utility. Available as static and variable fonts, with full italic companions throughout.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Micrograph as a sans serif that walks the line between classic and modern, offering subtle personality without sacrificing legibility. They highlight its tight spacing, compact proportions, very short descenders and clean mechanical aesthetic, noting its great versatility across all media.

name: Compressium
classification_notes: Compressium is an ultra-condensed display family derived by vertically scaling and recompressing an existing weight, with squared curves reinforcing its compact, high-impact character. It fits squarely in the display category given its extreme condensation and seven distinct weight grades.
summary: A seven-weight ultra-condensed display family built around squared curves and a compressed rhythm designed for strong typographic impact.
description: Evolved from a single distinctive weight that was vertically scaled and then recompressed to its original height, Compressium achieves an extreme condensation across seven styles from One through Seven. Squared curves reinforce the tight white-space rhythm throughout, making the family well-suited to headlines, posters and branding contexts that demand visual weight at large sizes. All seven static fonts are available individually or as part of a variable font that spans the full range.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Compressium as having evolved from the family's most distinctive weight, the Seven, which was first scaled vertically and then rescaled to match its original height to produce a more condensed result. The squared curves reinforce the compact rhythm by tightening white space, and the name itself reflects this process: seven ultra-condensed and powerful styles created to make a strong visual impact.

name: Shentox
classification_notes: Shentox is a contemporary sans-serif with clean, geometric construction and high legibility across weights, consistent with the geometric sans subcategory.
summary: A clean geometric sans in seven weights with italics and variable font support, suited to branding, editorial and large-scale display work.
description: Spanning Thin to Bold across 16 static styles plus Roman and Italic variable fonts, Shentox is built for legibility at scale with precise detailing across all weights. The family is broad enough to carry branding systems and editorial hierarchies without feeling generic. Real-world use includes EVE Online, Ready Player One and UEFA Europa League, pointing to its particular effectiveness in identity and campaign contexts.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Shentox as a highly legible contemporary font family useable for headlines and posters as well as branding and general editorial work. They note careful attention to details and high-end finishes that make it suitable for large-scale applications, with a Variable Font included in the full family or available as separate styles.

name: Majorant
classification_notes: Majorant is explicitly described as a geometric sans serif, with mathematical rigour informing its proportions and construction across its weight range.
summary: A geometric sans serif spanning eight weights from UltraThin to Black, with stylistic alternates that shift the family's tone from sharp and audacious to soft and classic.
description: Built on geometric principles, Majorant covers 18 styles including Roman and Italic Variable Fonts, making it usable across a wide range of applications. The extreme weights function as display cuts, with the UltraThin carrying a mathematical austerity and the Black an expressive weight. Stylistic alternates give designers meaningful control over the family's personality without switching typefaces.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Majorant as a geometric sans serif interpreted from a contemporary point of view, noting that its wide weight range makes it a multipurpose family. The extreme weights serve as display faces, from the mathematical rigour of the UltraThin to the expressive refinement of the Black. Several alternates allow the font to shift between sharp and audacious in its default form, or soft and classic via stylistic sets, and a Variable Font is included with the full family or available as a separate style.

name: Camber
classification_notes: Described as having a geometric base with squarish proportions and clean synthetic design, placing it firmly in the geometric sans category, though human sensitivity is introduced where strict geometry would otherwise falter.
summary: A squarish geometric sans with wider-than-average proportions, designed for versatility across display and text settings.
description: The last in Emtype's personal series of squarish sans typefaces, Camber takes a geometric foundation and softens it where strict construction would introduce visual noise. Its generous width addresses a gap in wider sans options, and the family spans seven weights from Thin to Bold with matching italics across all 14 styles. Suited to magazine, branding and web work where a clean but not purely mechanical sans is required.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Camber as the final entry in a personal series of squarish sans typefaces — a noiseless design with a geometric base and synthetic, clean character, but with human sensitivity introduced where geometry falls short. They emphasise its pragmatic approach, generous width and versatility across both display and text use, positioning it for magazine, branding and web applications.

name: Geogrotesque Mono
classification_notes: A fixed-width companion to a geometric grotesque, with rounded terminals that soften the mechanical character typical of monospaced typefaces.
summary: A fixed-width companion to Geogrotesque, in seven weights with a parallel underscored subfamily, keeping the rounded terminals of the original.
description: Designed as the monospaced counterpart to Emtype's Geogrotesque, this family carries over the rounded stroke endings that give the original its warmer, less clinical character. Fourteen styles cover seven weights from Thin to Bold, each mirrored by an underscored variant, with variable font versions included in the family. The combination of fixed-width spacing and geometric forms makes it suited to data tables and technical documentation as well as packaging or branding where a structured, slightly retro-technical tone is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Geogrotesque Mono as the fixed-width counterpart of Geogrotesque, noting that its rounded finish provides a warm appearance and makes it less hard or technological than typical monospaced designs. They position it as ideal for tables and drafts as well as for packaging or branding where a technical or classic feel is desired, and highlight it as the perfect match for their best-selling Geogrotesque. The family comes in 14 styles: seven weights plus seven underscored versions, with variable fonts included.

name: Goalking
classification_notes: Goalking blends humanist and geometric traits, with open apertures, tapered arch joints and rounded dots placing it closer to British humanist sans traditions such as Granby than to geometric or neo-grotesque models.
summary: A humanist sans drawing on British grotesque traditions, particularly Granby, with wide proportions, open apertures and tapered arch joints.
description: Goalking sits between humanist and geometric sans genres, taking cues from Stephenson Blake's Granby rather than Johnston's Underground in its open apertures and contrasted joints. Contemporary wide proportions, a generous x-height and spiky vertical terminals distinguish it from classical or handwriting-influenced humanist designs. The family covers eight weights from Thin to Black with matching italics, plus a variable font, making it a practical choice for modern editorial and branding work.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Goalking as blending humanist and geometric elements into a clean yet vigorous typeface. They cite its open apertures as giving it a British character closer to Granby than Johnston's Underground Sans, noting the contrast introduced by tapered arch joints and rounded dots. Wide contemporary proportions, spiky vertical terminals and a generous x-height make it both usable and legible, feeling familiar yet fresh for modern communication.

name: Aribau Rounded
classification_notes: A grotesque sans with softened, rounded terminals derived from Aribau Grotesk, designed for warmth without sacrificing legibility or professional register.
summary: Originally commissioned for a banking institution, this rounded grotesque pairs the clarity of Aribau Grotesk with softened terminals that add warmth without veering into playfulness.
description: Aribau Rounded is the companion to Aribau Grotesk, retaining its structural clarity while introducing rounded contours that give the family a more approachable tone. Sixteen styles span eight weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, plus a variable font, making it a practical choice for branding, annual reports and interface work. The design avoids the childlike associations common to rounded sans typefaces, keeping it credible across both editorial and corporate contexts.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Aribau Rounded as the friendly counterpart to Aribau Grotesk, originally commissioned for a banking institution. They note that its rounded contours add warmth while avoiding childlike or excessively playful connotations, and position its 16 styles as a friendly workhorse suited to applications ranging from annual reports to user interfaces and branding.

name: Approach Mono
classification_notes: A fixed-width variant of the Approach sans-serif family, low contrast and mechanically flavoured, designed explicitly for monospaced applications such as code and tabular data.
summary: The fixed-width companion to Approach, a low-contrast monospaced sans with a mechanical quality and more prominent punctuation than its proportional counterpart.
description: Derived from the Approach family, this monospaced variant retains the original's utilitarian character whilst introducing more visible punctuation suited to tabular layouts and code. Six weights run from Light to Black, covering practical needs across data-heavy documents, programming environments and editorial contexts where a cooler, more clinical voice is wanted. Despite its functional origins, it carries enough personality to work in branding, advertising and print communication beyond purely technical settings.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Approach Mono as the fixed-width version of Approach: a utilitarian, low-contrast font that is a bit mechanical but full of character. It shares its main features with the original but has more prominent, visible punctuation. While the obvious uses are tables, programming code or in-progress texts, Emtype suggest it can also bring an aseptic voice to brochures, advertising, identities and broader communication.

name: Inklination
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a grotesque, departing from genre conventions with a low x-height and long ascenders and descenders, placing it firmly in the grotesque sans-serif tradition.
summary: A grotesque sans with a deliberately low x-height and two sets of italics at 10° and 20° inclination, plus a five-weight monospaced companion.
description: Inklination breaks from contemporary grotesque conventions by adopting a low x-height paired with long ascenders and descenders, lending the text a light, airy quality suited to luxury and editorial applications. The family offers two italic cuts: a standard 10° inclination and a more aggressive 20° brutalist variant, giving designers meaningful tonal range within a single family. A five-weight monospaced subfamily rounds out the 20-font release.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Inklination as a grotesque that deliberately defies genre conventions by adopting a low x-height, which they say allows the text to breathe better than larger x-height typefaces, creating a sense of air and whitespace. Long ascenders and descenders contribute to a luxurious and refined character. The family includes two italic sets — one at 10° and a more brutalist cut at 20° — alongside a five-weight monospaced version, totalling 20 fonts.

name: Periódico
classification_notes: Designed with reference to 18th-century Spanish typographic engravings, the family shares the high contrast and calligraphic underpinning typical of transitional serifs, split across dedicated text and display optical sizes.
summary: Originally commissioned by Spanish daily newspaper ABC, this 30-style family draws on 18th-century Spanish typographic engravings without directly reviving any single model.
description: Built from scratch rather than as a revival, the family takes 18th-century Spanish engraving as a starting point for a distinctive yet functional newspaper face. Thirty styles split across Text and Display subfamilies cover five weights from Light to Bold in Text, and nine weights from Thin to UltraBlack in Display, each with italics. The family has since appeared in editorial contexts ranging from USPS stamps to Wired magazine.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Periódico, Spanish for newspaper, as originally commissioned by Spanish daily ABC. Rather than reviving or interpreting historical models, they drew on the most relevant details of old Spanish typographic engravings, mostly from the second half of the 18th century, to create an original family that is both distinctive and versatile.

name: Akkordeon Slab
classification_notes: A slab serif designed explicitly for ultra-display use, sharing the skeleton of the original Akkordeon grotesque family but recast with slab serifs for stronger impact at headline sizes.
summary: A slab-serif companion to Akkordeon, offered in fourteen numerically named steps and a variable font, built for high-impact headlines in sports, fashion and editorial contexts.
description: Sharing the same skeletal structure as the original Akkordeon, this slab variant applies a heavier, more assertive voice to the same underlying forms. Fourteen discrete styles progress from hairline-thin to extreme weight, each sold individually or as a complete family, with a variable font covering the full range in a single file. The Latin Extended character set makes it broadly usable across European languages.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Akkordeon Slab as the next step in a series of ultra display typefaces, sharing the same skeleton and spirit as the original Akkordeon but recast in slab form to deliver a stronger voice suited to sports, business, fashion and any situation demanding impactful headlines. A variable font is included with the full family or available as a separate style.

name: Realgar
classification_notes: Realgar blends geometric construction with grotesque conventions and humanist touches, placing it in an eclectic geometric grotesque subcategory. Its closed apertures and wide proportions are characteristic of grotesque influence, while the rounded dots and idiosyncratic numerals reflect geometric and humanist sensibilities.
summary: A wide-set geometric grotesque with closed apertures, rounded dots and idiosyncratic numerals that give it just enough character to avoid anonymity.
description: Realgar sits at the intersection of geometric, grotesque and humanist sans traditions, deploying wide proportions and closed apertures alongside rounded punctuation dots and distinctive numerals. The family runs across six weights from Regular to Black, each with a matching italic, and includes a variable font spanning the full range. Its eclectic construction makes it practical across editorial, branding and screen contexts where a neutral but characterful sans is needed.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Realgar as a generic and neutral typeface with enough personality to be different, but not so much as to feel unfamiliar. The name references an ancient natural pigment known for its vivid orange colour. They highlight its wide proportions, closed apertures and idiosyncratic numbers as distinguishing features, alongside rounded dots that give it a friendly and contemporary character. A variable font is included with the full family.

name: Classike
classification_notes: High contrast, squarish letterforms with a mechanical quality and strong vertical stress place this firmly in the display category; the Art Deco inspiration and geometric underpinning distinguish it from standard high-contrast serifs.
summary: A high-contrast, squarish display typeface drawing on Art Deco proportions with a mechanical edge, designed to pair with Geogrotesque.
description: Six weights from UltraLight to Bold, each with a matching italic, give Classike a versatile range for luxury branding, editorial headers and packaging where refinement and a period flavour are required. The letterforms share their proportions with Geogrotesque, lending the family a geometric rigour beneath its decorative surface. A variable font covering the full weight axis is included with the family purchase.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Classike as a high-contrast squarish display typeface inspired by the Art Deco period viewed through a modern lens. They position it as refined and elegant yet with a mechanical vibe, noting it pairs especially well with Geogrotesque, from which it inherited its proportions and soul. They suggest it adds an exclusive touch and helps enrich the graphic voice.

name: Chiaroscura
classification_notes: High contrast, sharp terminals and compact vertical proportions place this firmly in the display serif category, drawing on the chiaroscuro art technique of strong light-dark contrast as its typographic principle.
summary: A high-contrast display serif with sharp terminals and compact proportions, inspired by the chiaroscuro technique of light and shadow in visual art.
description: Fourteen styles span seven weights from ExtraLight to Black, each with a matching italic, giving designers flexibility across print and digital headline work. The sharp terminals and compressed vertical proportions lend it a refined, fashion-adjacent character suited to editorial mastheads, album artwork and luxury branding. Extended Latin character support includes diacritics, fractions and superscripts.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Chiaroscura as a display typeface inspired by the chiaroscuro art technique, characterised by high contrast, sharp terminals and compact vertical proportions. They position it as elegant and refined, suited to headlines in art, fashion, music and literature, and appropriate for any communication requiring a sophisticated typographic voice.

name: Geogrotesque ES
classification_notes: A geometric grotesque expanded series, spanning three width variants from Wide to Expanded, intended for both text and display use as part of the broader Geogrotesque superfamily.
summary: The expanded-width series of Geogrotesque, offering three progressively wider cuts from 120% to 200% of the standard width across seven weights.
description: Covering Wide, Extended and Expanded widths, this series complements the existing Geogrotesque Condensed cuts to form a complete width system. The Wide variant is proportioned for body text use, whilst Extended and Expanded are suited to headlines and display settings. All three widths come in seven weights from Thin to Bold, each with matching italics, totalling 42 styles.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe the Geogrotesque Expanded Series as comprising three widths — Wide, Extended and Expanded — ranging from 120% to 200% of the normal width. They note that since the original Geogrotesque is slightly condensed, the Wide variant works well for text, while Extended and Expanded are better suited to display sizes. Together with the Condensed series, these widths complete a full Geogrotesque superfamily width system.

name: Geogrotesque CS
classification_notes: A condensed series extension of the Geogrotesque family, which is a geometric grotesque; the CS variants maintain that geometric-grotesque character across three compressed width axes.
summary: An extension of the Geogrotesque family adding three narrower width series, ranging from Condensed to Extra Compressed across seven weights each with matching italics.
description: A 42-font condensed system built as an addition to the original Geogrotesque, spanning Condensed, Compressed and Extra Compressed widths down to roughly 55% of the base width. Each width series runs through seven weights from Thin to Bold with italics throughout, giving designers a fluid range of narrow cuts for headline and text use in publications. The Latin Extended glyph set includes fractions, superscripts, subscripts and a full range of diacritics for broad European language support.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Geogrotesque CS as an extended system that adds Condensed, Compressed and Extra Compressed members to the popular Geogrotesque family, maintaining the spirit of the original while varying widths from the standard down to 55% in the narrowest cut. They position the family as a great headline option for publications that also works in text, with the full superfamily offering a font for every design need.

name: Geogrotesque Cyrillic
classification_notes: A semi-modular geometric sans with subtle rounded terminals, placing it between a strict geometric grotesque and a humanist grotesque in character.
summary: The Cyrillic extension of Geogrotesque, a semi-modular geometric grotesque with subtle rounded terminals covering Latin Extended and Cyrillic scripts across 14 styles.
description: Built on the same formal principles as the original Geogrotesque, this extension adds a full Cyrillic character set alongside an expanded Latin, enabling use across more than 115 languages. Seven weights from Thin to Bold each have matching italics, and the rounded finish softens what would otherwise be a strictly geometric construction. The result sits comfortably in editorial, branding and interface work where a warmer geometric tone is needed.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Geogrotesque Cyrillic as a Cyrillic extension of the popular Geogrotesque, a semi-modular typeface with a subtle rounded finish. All characters follow the same formal principle with optical adjustments to suit text use, and while the construction is geometric or technological in nature, the rounded terminals lend warmth and approachability. The family covers Latin Extended and Cyrillic, supporting over 115 languages worldwide.

name: Geogrotesque Stencil
classification_notes: A stencil variant of the Geogrotesque geometric grotesque family, designed explicitly for display use with three cut widths to address practical printing constraints inherent to stencil typefaces.
summary: A stencil extension of the Geogrotesque family offering three cut widths to accommodate different print scales and material rigidities across seven weights.
description: Extending the Geogrotesque family into stencil territory, this variant addresses practical production problems by offering three versions of the cut — A, B and C — which allow designers to select the bridge width appropriate to the print size or material rigidity in use. The full family spans 42 styles across seven weights from Thin to Bold, each with a matching italic and all three cut variants. The result is a stencil system with real functional depth rather than a single decorative cut.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Geogrotesque Stencil as a member of the Geogrotesque family that, despite being conceived as a display typeface, attempts to solve common problems with stencil fonts. The three cut widths — A, B and C — improve performance at different print sizes and allow adjustment based on the rigidity of the material being used.

name: Geogrotesque Slab
classification_notes: A slab serif companion to the geometric grotesque Geogrotesque, retaining the same underlying geometric structure while adding bracketed serifs, placing it firmly in the geometric slab category.
summary: A slab serif companion to Geogrotesque, adding serifs to the same geometric skeleton across seven weights with matching italics.
description: Designed as a direct companion to Geogrotesque, this slab variant preserves the original's geometric proportions and approachable character while the added serifs introduce a firmer, more authoritative tone. The family spans 14 styles across seven weights from Thin to Bold, each with a corresponding italic. Broad Latin Extended glyph coverage, including ligatures, fractions and super- and subscript figures, makes it suited to editorial and corporate applications.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Geogrotesque Slab as the ideal companion to the popular Geogrotesque. The challenge was to create a fully recognisable font that functions as part of the existing family, with the slab version conveying the same message in a different way. The serifs bring security, confidence and firmness without sacrificing the original's approachability, and the addition gives the Geogrotesque family a solution for every design need.

name: Isotonic
classification_notes: Isotonic is a geometric-leaning sans-serif derived from the Ciutadella family, with open counters, a high x-height and soft corners that place it firmly in the humanised geometric sans category.
summary: A text-oriented spin-off of Ciutadella with open counters, a raised x-height and softened corners suited to sports, science and technology contexts.
description: Born from Emtype's Ciutadella, Isotonic was reworked with wider counters and a higher x-height to improve legibility at body sizes and on screen. The soft corners lend it an approachable character without sacrificing the clarity needed for data-heavy or editorial environments. Ten styles run across five weights from Light to Bold with matching italics throughout.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Isotonic as a spin-off project initially conceived as a text-oriented version of Ciutadella, one that has since developed independently. They opened the counters, raised the x-height and introduced soft corners, giving the typeface what they call charm and an appropriate voice for sports, science, tech and economy. Despite not being a strict text font, they note it performs surprisingly well at body sizes and on screen.

name: Ciutadella Slab
classification_notes: A slab serif companion to the Ciutadella sans-serif family, sharing its geometric construction while adding bracketed slab serifs suited to text use at intermediate sizes.
summary: A slab serif companion to Ciutadella, reworked for text use with alternate characters promoted to defaults for better readability at intermediate sizes.
description: Cittadella Slab extends the Ciutadella family with a slab serif cut suited to magazines, brochures and branding at text sizes. The former alternate 'a', 't' and '&' characters become the defaults here, shifting the register from display to text. Ten styles run across five weights from Light to Bold, each with a matching italic.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Ciutadella Slab as the serif counterpart of the popular Ciutadella font. The alternate characters from the original, including 'a', 't' and '&', are promoted to defaults, making it more suitable for text. They position it as a versatile addition to the Ciutadella family, appropriate for magazines, brochures and branding, and note it works at both headline and intermediate text sizes.

name: Ciutadella Display
classification_notes: Ciutadella Display follows the geometric humanist sans tradition of its parent family, pushed to extreme weight ranges for display use. The forms are clean and structured, typical of a geometric sans designed for large-size application.
summary: A display-specific extension of the Ciutadella family, stretching from Thin to UltraBlack across 12 styles for large-scale use.
description: Designed as the extreme counterpart to the original Ciutadella family, this display variant pushes weights to their limits in both directions. Six weights from Thin to UltraBlack each come with a matching italic, yielding 12 styles optimised for headlines and poster work. It integrates with Ciutadella, Ciutadella Rounded and Ciutadella Slab to form a broad type system.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Ciutadella Display as the extreme partner of the popular Ciutadella family, built for use at large sizes. The weight range has been taken to its limits in both directions, from Thin to UltraBlack, adding 12 new styles to the original family and creating an expanded, highly flexible system.

name: Relato Serif
classification_notes: Low contrast and muscular structure with terminals and curves drawn from humanist calligraphic traditions; uppercase based on Renaissance roman capitals and lowercase on humanist handwriting, placing it firmly in the humanist serif subcategory.
summary: A humanist serif with low contrast and calligraphic roots, drawing on Renaissance capitals and humanist handwriting for text and display use.
description: Combining a muscular, low-contrast structure with a variety of terminal strokes and the interplay of curves and semi-straight lines, it reads comfortably in long text while revealing more personality at display sizes. The uppercase draws on Renaissance roman capitals and the lowercase on humanist handwriting, giving the face a warmth that sits apart from more mechanical serifs. Five styles cover Regular, Regular Italic, Small Caps, SemiBold and Bold, all with broad Latin Extended glyph coverage including ligatures, fractions and super- and subscripts.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Relato Serif as a decidedly contemporary serif typeface with low contrast and a muscular structure suited to long text. At display sizes its range of terminal strokes and combination of curves and semi-straight lines give it a unique, personal expression. Its formal principles are rooted in calligraphic tradition: the uppercase draws on Renaissance roman capitals and the lowercase on humanist handwriting, lending it what they call a more human flavour.

name: Relato Sans
classification_notes: Described explicitly as humanistic with a contemporary cut, sharing proportional and structural DNA with its serif counterpart Relato Serif, placing it firmly in the humanist sans tradition.
summary: A humanist sans designed as the companion to Relato Serif, sharing its proportions and x-height across six weights from Light to Black.
description: Designed as the sans counterpart to Relato Serif, it shares proportions, x-height and italic construction with its serif companion, giving designers a coherent pairing for mixed-setting projects. Six weights from Light to Black each come with matching italics, totalling twelve styles. The character set covers Western and Central European Latin with a range of currency symbols, fractions and punctuation for general text use.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Relato Sans as the other face of Relato Serif, more austere and aseptic in character. They position it as a humanistic type with a contemporary cut made for general text use, with enough weight variety for demanding projects. It shares many characteristics with the serif version, including proportions, x-height, italic air-line construction and ornaments, reinforcing coherence between the two while establishing its own personality.

name: Dixplay
classification_notes: A pixel-grid display typeface designed on a 20px foundation, drawing from 8-bit video game aesthetics. Its construction is entirely rooted in pixel/bitmap conventions, placing it firmly in the display category with a pixel subcategory.
summary: A pixel typeface built on a 20px grid, drawing from 1980s video game aesthetics and refined with over 300 manual kerning pairs.
description: Conceived at 20px to allow greater structural freedom, Dixplay sits in an interesting position for a pixel font: it has been given serious typographic attention where most of its peers have none. Over 300 manually fitted kerning pairs are unusually meticulous for the genre, and the result works at screen sizes and their multiples with genuine grid integrity. Two weights, Regular and Black, cover Latin Extended characters including ligatures and a broad range of punctuation.
foundry_blurb: Emtype describe Dixplay as inspired by 1980s video game aesthetics, originally conceived for display use but functional in print as well. Designed at 20px to allow more manipulation freedom and to differentiate it from other pixel fonts, it is optimised for screen use at 20px and its multiples. Unlike typical pixel fonts, it includes over 300 manually crafted kerning pairs that fit precisely to the grid.
